title: Contains duplicate
description: Determine if an array contains any duplicate elements

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0, value=1. j=1: compare 1 with 2 → no match. j=2: compare 1 with 3 → no match. j=3: compare 1 with 1 → MATCH found. Return true immediately.

Step 1: i=0, value=1. j=1: 1 vs 2 → no. j=2: 1 vs 3 → no. j=3: 1 vs 4 → no. Step 2: i=1, value=2. j=2: 2 vs 3 → no. j=3: 2 vs 4 → no. Step 3: i=2, value=3. j=3: 3 vs 4 → no. Step 4: i=3, value=4 (no j loop as i+1=4 exceeds array length). All comparisons complete with no matches. Return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare each element with every other element in the array using nested loops to check for duplicates. Use this for small arrays or as a baseline approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to check if any element appears more than once in an array.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the duplicate detection problem by systematically comparing every element with every other element in the array. The intuition is straightforward: if any two different positions contain the same value, we have found a duplicate. This works because we're exhaustively checking all possible pairs (i, j) where i ≠ j. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each element as a potential 'reference' element, while the inner loop compares this reference against all subsequent elements. If a match is found, we immediately return true. If we complete all comparisons without finding a match, we return false. The time complexity is O(n²) because for an array of size n, we perform approximately n(n-1)/2 comparisons in the worst case. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables for iteration and comparison. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, working solution. Common mistakes include: comparing an element with itself (i == j), which would incorrectly flag all arrays as having duplicates; not optimizing the inner loop to start from i+1, which would double the comparisons; and forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays. The real code pattern involves careful loop boundary management and early termination when a duplicate is found.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, value=1. j=1: compare 1 with 2 → no match. j=2: compare 1 with 3 → no match. j=3: compare 1 with 1 → MATCH found. Return true immediately.

Step 1: i=0, value=1. j=1: 1 vs 2 → no. j=2: 1 vs 3 → no. j=3: 1 vs 4 → no. Step 2: i=1, value=2. j=2: 2 vs 3 → no. j=3: 2 vs 4 → no. Step 3: i=2, value=3. j=3: 3 vs 4 → no. Step 4: i=3, value=4 (no j loop as i+1=4 exceeds array length). All comparisons complete with no matches. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pairwise Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: The nested loop structure that compares each element with all subsequent elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core comparison logic of the brute force approach. The intuition is to systematically generate all unique pairs of indices (i, j) where i < j, ensuring each pair is checked exactly once without redundancy. This works because for duplicate detection, we only need to check if any two different positions have equal values, and the order doesn't matter (comparing position 1 with position 2 is the same as comparing 2 with 1). The algorithm uses an outer loop with index i ranging from 0 to n-2, and an inner loop with index j ranging from i+1 to n-1. For each pair (i, j), we compare arr[i] with arr[j]. If they're equal, we've found a duplicate and can terminate early. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n²) in the worst case when no duplicates exist, as it performs n(n-1)/2 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the complete comparison mechanism—once we have this working, we just need to wrap it with proper input handling and return values. Common mistakes include: starting the inner loop at 0 instead of i+1 (which would compare each pair twice and waste time), using i <= j instead of i < j (which would compare elements with themselves), and not handling the case when i reaches n-1 correctly (the inner loop should not execute when i = n-1). The real code pattern emphasizes clean loop boundaries and early exit optimization.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: j=1 compare 10 with 20 → no match. j=2 compare 10 with 30 → no match. i=1: j=2 compare 20 with 30 → no match. i=2: loop ends (i=2, n-1=2). All comparisons complete with no matches.
n=3. i=0: j=1 compare 5 with 5 → MATCH found. Return immediately with duplicate detected at indices 0 and 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element-to-Element Equality Check
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing two array elements for equality.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the simple but essential operation of determining whether two given elements from an array are equal. The intuition is that duplicate detection ultimately reduces to answering the question: 'Are these two values the same?' This works because if any two elements at different positions have identical values, we have found a duplicate. The algorithm is straightforward: given two indices i and j into an array arr, retrieve arr[i] and arr[j], then compare them using the equality operator (==). If they match, return true (duplicate found); otherwise, return false. The time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the pairwise comparison loop (Level 1) by being the atomic operation repeated within the nested loops. Without this, the higher-level algorithms wouldn't have a way to identify duplicates. Common mistakes include: confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), which would modify values instead of comparing them; comparing indices instead of values (i == j instead of arr[i] == arr[j]); and not considering type consistency (though in this problem, we assume homogeneous arrays). The real code pattern is simple but must be precise, as it forms the building block for all comparison-based duplicate detection methods.

examples.trace: Step 1: Retrieve arr[0] = 10. Step 2: Retrieve arr[2] = 30. Step 3: Compare 10 == 30. Step 4: Result is false (not equal).
Step 1: Retrieve arr[0] = 5. Step 2: Retrieve arr[1] = 5. Step 3: Compare 5 == 5. Step 4: Result is true (equal).

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Iteration
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements by index and looping through them systematically.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers how to access individual elements in an array using indices and how to iterate through arrays using loops. The intuition is that to compare elements, we first need to retrieve them, and to check all possible pairs, we need to systematically visit each position. This works because arrays provide random access—we can directly access any element if we know its index (position). The algorithm involves: understanding array indexing (typically 0-based, where the first element is at index 0), using a loop variable (like i) to traverse indices from 0 to n-1, and accessing elements with arr[i]. For nested loops, we use two loop variables (i and j) to create coordinate pairs. The time complexity depends on how many indices we visit—for a single loop through n elements, it's O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration variables. This feeds into the core logic (Level 2) by providing the mechanism to get the elements to compare. Without proper indexing and iteration, we couldn't implement any array algorithm. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., looping while i <= n instead of i < n, which would cause an out-of-bounds access), confusing the index with the value (thinking arr[i] gives the index rather than the value), and not initializing loop variables correctly. The real code pattern emphasizes careful loop construction and boundary checking, which is fundamental to all array processing.

examples.input: Array: [10, 20, 30], iterate and print each element
examples.trace: n=3. i=0: access arr[0]=10, print 10. i=1: access arr[1]=20, print 20. i=2: access arr[2]=30, print 30. i=3: loop condition i<3 fails, exit.
Index 0: arr[0] = 5. Index 1: arr[1] = 5. Index 2: arr[2] = 8.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Value Comparison
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of checking if two values are equal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two values to determine if they are identical. The intuition is that at the heart of duplicate detection is the ability to recognize when two things are the same. This works because in programming, we can use comparison operators (like == in most languages) to evaluate whether two expressions result in the same value. The algorithm is simple: take two inputs, a and b, and evaluate the expression a == b. If true, the values are equal; if false, they are different. The time and space complexity are both O(1), as it's a constant-time operation. This feeds into array indexing and iteration (Level 3) by being the operation we perform on the retrieved elements. Without value comparison, we couldn't progress to element-to-element checks. Common mistakes include: using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), which would change a variable's value rather than test equality; comparing objects by reference instead of value in languages where that matters (though for primitives like integers, it's straightforward); and not understanding that comparison returns a boolean (true/false) result. The real code pattern is universal across programming languages and forms the basis for conditional logic and search algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Take first value: 10. Step 2: Take second value: 20. Step 3: Evaluate 10 == 20. Step 4: Result is false (not equal).
Step 1: Take first value: 5. Step 2: Take second value: 5. Step 3: Evaluate 5 == 5. Step 4: Result is true (equal).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: what an array is and how it stores data.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic concept of an array as a data structure. The intuition is that an array is a collection of items stored in contiguous memory locations, allowing us to group related data together. This works because arrays provide a way to manage multiple values under a single name, with each value accessible via an index (a numerical position). Think of it like a row of lockers: each locker has a number (index) and contains an item (value). The algorithm for understanding involves: declaring an array (e.g., arr = [1, 2, 3]), knowing its size (length, often n), and understanding that indices typically start at 0 (so the first element is arr[0]). There's no time/space analysis here as it's a conceptual foundation. This feeds into basic operations (Level 4) by providing the context where values come from—without arrays, we'd just have isolated values to compare. Common mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1 (which would cause off-by-one errors), confusing the array itself with its elements, and not realizing that arrays can hold any data type (though here we focus on integers). The real code pattern is about creating and accessing arrays, which is the first step in handling collections of data in programming.

examples.output: Array with 3 elements: index 0=10, index 1=20, index 2=30
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify the array: [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Count elements: 3. Step 3: List each with index: position 0 has value 10, position 1 has value 20, position 2 has value 30.
Step 1: Array is [5, 5, 8]. Step 2: Indices: 0=5, 1=5, 2=8. Step 3: Second element means index 1 (since we start counting from 0). Step 4: Value at index 1 is 5.

testCases.input: Array [1, 2, 1], list all indices and values

approaches.name: Sorting O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then scan linearly to check if adjacent elements are equal. This is efficient for medium-sized arrays where sorting is acceptable.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting implementation

ladders.title: Full Problem — Contains Duplicate via Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array then check adjacent elements for duplicates
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the 'contains duplicate' problem by first sorting the array, then scanning through it to check if any two consecutive elements are equal. The intuition is that sorting brings identical elements next to each other, making duplicate detection trivial with a single linear pass. After sorting (O(n log n)), we iterate through the array comparing each element with its neighbor. If we find any pair where nums[i] == nums[i+1], we return True. If we complete the scan without finding duplicates, we return False. The space complexity depends on the sorting implementation: O(1) for in-place sorts like heapsort, O(n) for mergesort. This approach is particularly effective for medium-sized arrays where the O(n log n) time is acceptable and we want to avoid the O(n) space of hash-based solutions. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays, not considering that sorting modifies the original array (which might be undesirable), and off-by-one errors in the loop bounds. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the sorted array scanning as its core subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort array → [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
Step 2: Initialize i = 0
Step 3: Compare nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=1 → equal → duplicate found
Step 4: Return True

Step 1: Sort array → [2, 4, 5, 7, 9]
Step 2: Initialize i = 0
Step 3: Compare nums[0]=2 with nums[1]=4 → not equal
Step 4: i = 1, compare nums[1]=4 with nums[2]=5 → not equal
Step 5: i = 2, compare nums[2]=5 with nums[3]=7 → not equal
Step 6: i = 3, compare nums[3]=7 with nums[4]=9 → not equal
Step 7: Loop completes without finding duplicates
Step 8: Return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Scan Sorted Array for Adjacent Duplicates
ladders.desc: Check if any two consecutive elements in a sorted array are equal
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine takes a sorted array as input and returns whether it contains any duplicate elements by checking adjacent pairs. The intuition is that in a sorted array, identical elements are guaranteed to be consecutive, so we only need to compare each element with its immediate neighbor. The algorithm iterates from index 0 to len(nums)-2, comparing nums[i] with nums[i+1]. If any pair is equal, we immediately return True. If we complete the loop without finding equal pairs, we return False. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra memory. This feeds into L0 by providing the duplicate detection logic after the array has been sorted. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (going up to len(nums)-1 instead of len(nums)-2, which would cause index out of bounds), not handling empty or single-element arrays properly, and forgetting that the array must be sorted for this approach to work. The real code pattern is straightforward: for i in range(len(nums)-1): if nums[i] == nums[i+1]: return True; return False.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i = 0, compare nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=1 → equal
Step 2: Duplicate found, return True

Step 1: i = 0, compare nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=2 → not equal
Step 2: i = 1, compare nums[1]=2 with nums[2]=3 → not equal
Step 3: i = 2, compare nums[2]=3 with nums[3]=4 → not equal
Step 4: i = 3, compare nums[3]=4 with nums[4]=5 → not equal
Step 5: Loop completes, return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Adjacent Comparison in Ordered Sequence
ladders.desc: Compare each element with its immediate neighbor in a sequence
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating through any sequence and comparing each element with the one that comes immediately after it. The intuition is that many problems can be solved by examining relationships between consecutive elements. The algorithm maintains an index i that goes from 0 to len(seq)-2, and at each step compares seq[i] with seq[i+1]. Depending on the problem, we might check for equality, greater-than, less-than, or other relationships. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear traversal. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the fundamental pattern of adjacent comparison, which L1 specializes to duplicate detection in sorted arrays. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop termination conditions, not considering edge cases like empty sequences or single-element sequences, and modifying the sequence while iterating through it. The real code pattern is: for i in range(len(seq)-1): # compare seq[i] and seq[i+1]. This pattern appears in many algorithms including finding peaks, checking if array is sorted, and detecting duplicates.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i = 0, compare seq[0]=5 with seq[1]=3
Step 2: i = 1, compare seq[1]=3 with seq[2]=8
Step 3: i = 2, compare seq[2]=8 with seq[3]=2
Step 4: Loop completes after 3 comparisons

Step 1: i = 0, compare seq[0]=10 with seq[1]=20
Step 2: Loop completes after 1 comparison

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index
ladders.desc: Iterate through array elements using index positions
ladders.explanation: This building block involves sequentially accessing each element in an array using its index position. The intuition is that arrays provide random access to elements via indices, allowing us to process elements in any order, though sequential processing is most common. The algorithm typically uses a loop with an index variable i that starts at 0 and increments by 1 until it reaches len(array)-1. At each iteration, we access array[i] to read or modify the element. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) if we only use the index variable. This feeds into L2 by providing the fundamental mechanism to access consecutive elements for comparison. Common mistakes include going out of bounds (accessing array[len(array)]), using the wrong increment (skipping elements), and confusing zero-based indexing with one-based indexing. The real code pattern is: for i in range(len(array)): # process array[i]. This is one of the most fundamental patterns in programming and appears in virtually every algorithm that processes collections of data.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i = 0, access array[0] = 10
Step 2: i = 1, access array[1] = 20
Step 3: i = 2, access array[2] = 30
Step 4: Loop terminates as i reaches 3 (len(array))

Step 1: i = 0, access array[0] = 5
Step 2: Loop terminates as i reaches 1 (len(array))

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing a specific element in an array by providing its position (index). The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, with each element having a numerical address called an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The operation is typically written as array[index], which returns the value stored at that position. Time complexity is O(1) for random access. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional memory is needed. This feeds into L3 by providing the fundamental operation needed to retrieve elements during traversal. Common mistakes include using indices outside the valid range (less than 0 or greater than or equal to len(array)), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, and forgetting that arrays are typically fixed-size data structures. The real code pattern is simple: element = array[2] would retrieve the third element. This operation is fundamental to working with arrays and appears in virtually every algorithm.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify array = [5, 10, 15]
Step 2: Identify index = 1
Step 3: Access element at position 1
Step 4: Return array[1] = 10

Step 1: Identify array = [a, b, c]
Step 2: Identify index = 0
Step 3: Access element at position 0
Step 4: Return array[0] = a

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections accessible by position
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces arrays as data structures that store multiple elements in a specific order, with each element having a numerical position called an index. The intuition is that arrays allow us to organize related data together and access individual elements efficiently. An array is defined as a contiguous block of memory containing elements of the same type. Elements are arranged sequentially, with the first element at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The length of an array is fixed when created (in many languages). To access an element, we use the syntax array[index]. Arrays support basic operations like reading elements (O(1)), updating elements (O(1)), and iterating through all elements (O(n)). This feeds into L4 by establishing what arrays are and how indexing works. Common beginner mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, trying to access indices beyond the array size, and confusing arrays with other data structures like lists. The real code pattern for creating an array might be: arr = [1, 2, 3]. This is the absolute foundation for understanding how to work with collections of data in programming.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify the array has 3 elements
Step 2: First element (position 0) is 'apple'
Step 3: Second element (position 1) is 'banana'
Step 4: Third element (position 2) is 'cherry'
Step 5: Array length is 3, valid indices are 0, 1, 2

Step 1: Identify the array has 2 elements
Step 2: First element (position 0) is 10
Step 3: Second element (position 1) is 20
Step 4: Array length is 2, valid indices are 0, 1

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash set to store seen elements while iterating through the array; if an element is already in the set, a duplicate exists. This is optimal for most cases due to linear time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Duplicates Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if an array contains duplicate elements using a hash set.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that we need to efficiently track which elements we've already encountered while scanning through the array. A hash set provides O(1) average-case time for both insertion and lookup operations, making it ideal for this purpose. As we iterate through each element, we check if it already exists in our set. If it does, we've found a duplicate and can return true immediately. If we complete the iteration without finding any duplicates, we return false. This approach is optimal for most cases because it achieves linear time complexity O(n) where n is the number of elements in the array, and linear space complexity O(n) for storing the hash set. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hash set. 2. Iterate through each element in the array. 3. For each element, check if it exists in the set. 4. If it exists, return true (duplicate found). 5. If it doesn't exist, add it to the set. 6. After iterating through all elements, return false (no duplicates found). This connects to the broader problem of duplicate detection by providing an efficient solution that works for any data type that can be hashed. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty arrays (should return false), using a data structure with worse time complexity (like checking against all previous elements for each element, which would be O(n²)), or forgetting to add elements to the set after checking them. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def contains_duplicate(nums): seen = set(); for num in nums: if num in seen: return True; seen.add(num); return False.

examples.trace: Initialize seen = {}. Process element 1: 1 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1}. Process element 2: 2 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1, 2}. Process element 3: 3 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1, 2, 3}. Process element 1: 1 in seen → return true.

Initialize seen = {}. Process element 1: 1 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1}. Process element 2: 2 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1, 2}. Process element 3: 3 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1, 2, 3}. Process element 4: 4 not in seen → add to seen, seen = {1, 2, 3, 4}. After all elements, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Add to Hash Set
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that checks if an element exists in a set and adds it if not.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the heart of the duplicate detection algorithm. The intuition is that we need to maintain a collection of seen elements and efficiently determine if a new element has been seen before. A hash set is perfect for this because it supports average O(1) time for membership checks and insertions. The algorithm: 1. Take an element and a hash set as input. 2. Check if the element is in the set. 3. If it is, return true (indicating a duplicate). 4. If it isn't, add the element to the set and return false (indicating no duplicate yet). This runs in O(1) average time per operation and uses O(1) extra space per element added. It connects to the full problem by being the repeated operation in the main loop—each iteration of the array calls this sub-routine. Common mistakes include not returning the correct boolean value, modifying the set incorrectly, or using a data structure with slower operations. The real code pattern: def check_and_add(element, seen): if element in seen: return True; seen.add(element); return False.

examples.trace: Check if 5 is in set {1, 2, 3, 4}: 5 not in set → add 5 to set, set becomes {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, return false.
Check if 3 is in set {1, 2, 3, 4}: 3 in set → return true (set remains {1, 2, 3, 4}).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Membership Check in Hash Set
ladders.desc: Determine if a given element exists in a hash set.
ladders.explanation: The core logic here is performing a membership check in a hash set, which is fundamental to the duplicate detection approach. The intuition is that hash sets use hashing to map elements to indices in an underlying array, allowing for average O(1) time lookups. When we check if an element is in the set, the hash function computes a hash code for the element, which points to a bucket in the set's internal storage. Then, it checks if the element equals any element in that bucket (handling collisions if necessary). This efficiency is why hash sets are preferred over linear searches in arrays or lists, which would take O(n) time. The algorithm: 1. Compute the hash of the element. 2. Use the hash to locate the corresponding bucket in the set. 3. Check if the element is present in that bucket. 4. Return true if found, false otherwise. This has O(1) average time complexity and O(1) space for the check itself. It connects to the key sub-routine as the first step—before adding an element, we need to check if it's already a member. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the time complexity (it's average O(1), but worst-case O(n) with many collisions), or using an incorrect data type that isn't hashable. The real code pattern: def is_in_set(element, seen): return element in seen.

examples.trace: Compute hash for 3 (e.g., hash(3) = 3). Check bucket 3 in set: contains 3 → return true.
Compute hash for 5 (e.g., hash(5) = 5). Check bucket 5 in set: empty or doesn't contain 5 → return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array and Track State
ladders.desc: Loop through an array while maintaining a data structure to track seen elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over an array and using an auxiliary data structure to store information about the elements processed so far. The intuition is that many algorithms require keeping track of state across iterations, and in this case, we need to remember which elements we've encountered to detect duplicates. The step-by-step process: 1. Initialize an empty data structure (e.g., a set). 2. For each element in the array: a. Perform an operation based on the current state (e.g., check if element is in the set). b. Update the state if necessary (e.g., add element to set). 3. After the loop, use the final state to determine the result. This has time complexity O(n) for the iteration and space complexity O(n) for the auxiliary structure. It connects to the core logic by providing the iterative framework where membership checks occur. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop, not initializing the state properly, or incorrectly updating the state. The real code pattern: seen = set(); for element in array: # operation with seen; # update seen if needed.

examples.input: Array: [1, 2, 3], Operation: Print element if not in set, then add to set
examples.trace: Initialize seen = {}. Element 1: 1 not in seen → print 1, add to seen, seen = {1}. Element 2: 2 not in seen → print 2, add to seen, seen = {1, 2}. Element 3: 3 not in seen → print 3, add to seen, seen = {1, 2, 3}.

examples.input: Array: [1, 2, 1], Operation: Print element if in set, then add to set
examples.trace: Initialize seen = {}. Element 1: 1 not in seen → don't print, add to seen, seen = {1}. Element 2: 2 not in seen → don't print, add to seen, seen = {1, 2}. Element 1: 1 in seen → print 1, add to seen, seen = {1, 2}.

testCases.input: Array: [], Operation: Count elements not in set, add to set
Array: [5], Operation: Count elements not in set, add to set
Array: [1, 2, 3], Operation: Count elements not in set, add to set
Array: [1, 1, 1], Operation: Count elements not in set, add to set
Array: [10, 20, 30], Operation: Sum elements not in set, add to set
Array: [10, 10, 20], Operation: Sum elements not in set, add to set
Array: [0, 1, 0], Operation: List elements in set before adding
Array: [2, 4, 6], Operation: List elements in set after adding
Array: [3, 3, 4], Operation: Check if element in set, return true if any duplicate
Array: [3, 4, 5], Operation: Check if element in set, return true if any duplicate

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Add Element to Hash Set
ladders.desc: Insert a new element into a hash set if it is not already present.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves adding an element to a hash set, which is a fundamental action in many algorithms. The intuition is that hash sets store unique elements, so adding an element ensures no duplicates within the set itself. Under the hood, when we add an element: 1. The hash function computes a hash code for the element. 2. This hash code determines the bucket (index) in the set's internal array. 3. If the bucket is empty, the element is placed there. 4. If the bucket already contains elements (due to collisions), it checks if the element is already present; if not, it adds the element to the bucket (using techniques like chaining or open addressing). This operation has average O(1) time complexity and uses O(1) extra space per addition. It connects to the building block as one way to update the state during iteration. Common mistakes include adding duplicates (which hash sets prevent automatically), or using a data structure that doesn't enforce uniqueness. The real code pattern: seen.add(element).

examples.trace: Compute hash for 5 (e.g., hash(5) = 5). Check bucket 5: empty → add 5 to bucket 5. Set becomes {1, 2, 3, 5}.
Compute hash for 2 (e.g., hash(2) = 2). Check bucket 2: contains 2 → no change, set remains {1, 2, 3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Hash Sets and Uniqueness
ladders.desc: Learn what a hash set is and how it stores unique elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces hash sets, which are data structures that store a collection of unique elements. The intuition is that in many problems, we need to keep track of items without duplicates, and hash sets provide an efficient way to do this. A hash set works by using a hash function to map each element to a specific index (bucket) in an internal array. When you add an element, it computes the hash, checks if the element is already in the corresponding bucket (to ensure uniqueness), and adds it if not. Key properties: 1. Uniqueness: No duplicate elements are allowed. 2. Fast operations: Average O(1) time for add, remove, and lookup. 3. Unordered: Elements are not stored in any particular order (though some implementations may maintain order). This connects to the basic operation as the underlying mechanism for adding elements. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists (which allow duplicates) or not understanding that hash sets rely on hashable elements (e.g., integers, strings, but not lists unless made immutable). The real code pattern: seen = set() creates an empty hash set.

examples.trace: Initialize empty set: {}. Add 1: hash(1) → bucket, add 1, set = {1}. Add 2: hash(2) → bucket, add 2, set = {1, 2}. Add 3: hash(3) → bucket, add 3, set = {1, 2, 3}.
Initialize empty set: {}. Add 1: hash(1) → bucket, add 1, set = {1}. Add 1 again: hash(1) → bucket, 1 already present, no change, set = {1}. Add 2: hash(2) → bucket, add 2, set = {1, 2}.
